title: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Incidence, Epidemiology, and Risk Factors of Diverticular Disease
items.content: Diverticulosis affects half of the population over 60 in the U.S., with 5% developing acute diverticular disease. Recurrent symptoms occur in 10-25%, and up to 10% require surgery due to complications. The condition is costly, leading to elective colon resections. Risk factors include NSAIDs, aspirin, steroids, opioids, smoking, and a sedentary lifestyle.
keyPoints: Diverticulosis affects ~50% of individuals >60 years in the U.S.
~5% develop acute diverticular disease
10-25% experience recurrent symptoms
Up to 10% require surgery due to complications
Risk factors: NSAIDs, aspirin, steroids, opioids, smoking, sedentary lifestyle
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Anatomy and Pathophysiology of Diverticula
items.content: True diverticula involve the entire bowel wall, while pseudodiverticula involve only mucosa and submucosa. Pseudodiverticula are common in the sigmoid colon due to high-pressure zones. Vasa recta compression or erosion can lead to perforation or bleeding.
keyPoints: True diverticulum: herniation of entire bowel wall
Pseudodiverticulum: protrusion of mucosa and submucosa
Common in sigmoid colon due to high-pressure zones
Vasa recta compression/erosion can cause perforation or bleeding
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Presentation and Management of Diverticular Bleeding
items.content: Hemorrhage from colonic diverticula is the most common cause of hematochezia in patients over 60, affecting 20% with diverticulosis. Risk factors include hypertension, atherosclerosis, anticoagulant use, NSAIDs, obesity, and diabetes. Most bleeds are self-limited, but rebleeding risk is 25%.
keyPoints: Most common cause of hematochezia in patients >60
20% with diverticulosis experience gastrointestinal bleeding
Risk factors: hypertension, atherosclerosis, anticoagulants, NSAIDs, obesity, diabetes
Lifetime rebleeding risk is 25%
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Management of Acute Diverticular Bleeding
items.content: In stable patients with acute diverticular bleeding, angiography is the preferred initial management. If mesenteric angiography localizes the bleeding site, coil embolization can successfully occlude the vessel in 80% of cases. This approach has a low risk of colonic ischemia (<10%) and rebleeding (<25%). Long-term outcomes show that over 50% of patients achieve definitive treatment with highly selective angiography.
keyPoints: Angiography is preferred for stable patients with acute diverticular bleeding.
Coil embolization success rate: 80% if the bleeding site is localized.
Risk of colonic ischemia <10%, rebleeding <25% with highly selective coil embolization.
>50% of patients achieve definitive treatment with highly selective angiography over 40 months.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Endoscopic and Surgical Interventions for Diverticular Bleeding
items.content: If the bleeding site can be localized, colonoscopic ligation with banding, placement of a detachable snare, or over-the-scope hemoclip are effective in achieving hemostasis. These methods help prevent rebleeding or emergent surgery. If these measures fail or if the patient is unstable and requires large-volume transfusion, surgical intervention is recommended.
keyPoints: Colonoscopic ligation with banding, detachable snare, or over-the-scope hemoclip can achieve hemostasis.
These methods prevent rebleeding or emergent surgery.
Surgery is indicated if endoscopic measures fail or the patient is unstable and requires large-volume transfusion.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Staging of Complicated Diverticular Disease
items.content: The Hinchey classification system stages perforated diverticular disease to predict surgical outcomes. It includes modifications for phlegmon or early abscess (Hinchey stage Ia). Fistula formation, such as colovaginal fistulas, is more common in women post-hysterectomy and presents with stool passage through the skin or vagina or pneumaturia.
keyPoints: The Hinchey classification system stages perforated diverticular disease.
Includes phlegmon or early abscess (Hinchey stage Ia).
Colovaginal fistulas are more common post-hysterectomy, presenting with stool passage through the skin/vagina or pneumaturia.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.title: Antibiotic Use in Uncomplicated Diverticulitis
items.content: Routine antibiotics are not recommended for uncomplicated diverticulitis as they do not reduce symptom resolution time, recurrence rates, or risk of complications. Antibiotics should be reserved for immunocompromised patients and those with signs of complicated disease.
keyPoints: Two large trials (AVOD and Diabolo) and a meta-analysis show no benefit in using antibiotics for uncomplicated diverticulitis.
Antibiotics are indicated for immunocompromised patients or those with extensive inflammation, risk factors for progression, or CT findings of complicated disease.
Risk factors for progression include ASA classification III/IV, >5 days of symptoms, and elevated CRP or WBC count.

Recommended antibiotics: third-generation cephalosporin (or ciprofloxacin if allergic) plus metronidazole, or single-agent therapy with a third-generation penicillin like IV piperacillin or oral penicillin/clavulanic acid.
Typical antibiotic course duration is 5 days.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.title: Oral vs. Intravenous Antibiotics for Uncomplicated Diverticulitis
items.content: For uncomplicated diverticulitis, oral antibiotics are as effective as intravenous therapy and can be safely administered at home after a brief observation period in the emergency department.
keyPoints: Study indicates no difference in recovery time or disease progression between IV and oral antibiotic treatments for uncomplicated diverticulitis.

Oral antibiotics are recommended after 6-hour observation if patients meet criteria: no complicated disease, not immunocompromised, no significant comorbidities, adequate social support, and stable during observation.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.title: Management of Uncomplicated Diverticulitis: Diet and Medical Therapies
items.content: For patients with uncomplicated diverticular disease, a limited diet is recommended until pain resolves. Mesalazine can reduce symptoms and recurrence in symptomatic uncomplicated diverticular disease (SUDD), but lacks benefit when objective inflammation markers are considered. Rifaximin shows promise by reducing recurrent symptoms by 30% compared to fiber alone.
keyPoints: Limited diet until pain resolution for uncomplicated diverticulitis
Mesalazine reduces symptoms and recurrence in SUDD, no benefit with CRP/computerized imaging
Rifaximin associated with 30% less frequent recurrent symptoms compared to fiber alone
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.title: Indications for Colonoscopy in Diverticular Disease
items.content: Colonoscopy is recommended ~6 weeks after the first episode of uncomplicated diverticular disease and following complicated diverticular disease. Despite a low overall prevalence of colon cancer (<2%) in these patients, the incidence increases to 6-8% with complicated diverticulitis, warranting endoscopic evaluation before surgery.
keyPoints: Colonoscopy ~6 weeks post-first episode of uncomplicated diverticular disease
Endoscopic evaluation after complicated diverticular disease
Low overall colon cancer prevalence (<2%) in diverticular patients; higher incidence (6-8%) with complicated cases
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.title: Indications for Surgical Therapy in Diverticular Disease
items.content: Surgical therapy is generally indicated for low-surgical-risk patients with complicated diverticular disease. Elective surgical resection can improve quality of life and cost-effectiveness over conservative management, particularly noted in the DIRECT trial.
keyPoints: Low-surgical-risk patients with complicated diverticular disease should undergo surgery.
Elective surgical resection improves quality of life and is more cost-effective at 5 years compared to conservative management (DIRECT trial).
Laparoscopic surgery for refractory or complicated diverticular disease reduces recurrent symptoms by 50% compared to conservative treatment (LASER trial).
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Surgical Options and Outcomes Based on Hinchey Classification
items.content: The surgical management of diverticular disease varies based on the Hinchey classification, with options ranging from percutaneous drainage to resection with anastomosis. The choice depends on the severity of the condition.
keyPoints: Hinchey stage Ia may be managed with antibiotics or followed by resection with anastomosis after symptom resolution.
Stages Ib and II are managed with percutaneous drainage followed by resection with anastomosis.
CT-guided percutaneous drainage is recommended for diverticular abscesses >3 cm; those <5 cm may resolve with antibiotics alone.
Hinchey stage III management includes Hartmann's procedure or primary anastomosis and proximal diversion, with laparoscopic lavage as a debated option due to reoperation risk.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Considerations for Minimally Invasive Surgery in Diverticular Disease
items.content: Minimally invasive surgery offers benefits over open techniques, including early discharge and fewer complications. However, specific considerations apply based on the Hinchey stage.
keyPoints: Benefits of minimally invasive resection include early discharge (by at least 1 day), less narcotic use, fewer postoperative complications, and earlier return to work.
Laparoscopic washout for Hinchey III patients has a threefold increased risk of recurrent peritonitis requiring reoperation.
Robotic surgery is associated with lower conversion rates to open procedures.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Recurrence in Diverticular Disease Post-Surgery
items.content: Recurrent symptoms after surgical resection for diverticular disease occur in 10% of patients. Inadequate surgical resection, particularly retaining a diseased segment of the rectosigmoid colon, doubles the risk of recurrence. Additionally, irritable bowel syndrome can mimic or exacerbate post-surgical symptoms.
keyPoints: Recurrent abdominal symptoms occur in 10% of patients after surgery for diverticular disease.
Inadequate surgical resection is linked to higher recurrence rates; retaining a diseased segment doubles the incidence.
Irritable bowel syndrome may cause recurrence of initial symptoms, leading to poorer functional outcomes.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.title: Key Epidemiological Insights into Rectal Prolapse
items.content: Rectal prolapse is significantly more prevalent in women than men, with a peak incidence in women over 60 years. It often coexists with other pelvic floor disorders and certain conditions like cystic fibrosis in children.
keyPoints: Rectal prolapse occurs six times more frequently in women than in men.
Incidence peaks in women over 60 years of age.
20% of children with rectal prolapse have cystic fibrosis; all should undergo a sweat chloride test.
Associated conditions include urinary incontinence, rectocele, cystocele, and enterocele.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.title: Management of Rectal Prolapse: Surgical vs Non-Surgical Approaches
items.content: The management of rectal prolapse primarily involves surgical correction, as non-surgical methods like biofeedback are only successful in one-third of patients. Surgical options include transabdominal and transperineal approaches, with the former associated with lower recurrence rates.
keyPoints: Biofeedback is effective in only 33% of rectal prolapse cases.
Transabdominal approaches have lower recurrence rates (<10%).
Ventrolateral rectopexy improves pelvic floor function without requiring sigmoid resection.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.title: Surgical Techniques for Rectal Prolapse: Transabdominal vs Transperineal
items.content: Transabdominal approaches, such as presacral suture or mesh rectopexy, are preferred due to lower recurrence rates. However, transperineal methods like the Altmeier procedure may be suitable for patients with significant comorbidities.
keyPoints: Transabdominal procedures include presacral suture and ventrolateral rectopexy.
Recurrence rates for transabdominal approaches are <10%.
Transperineal methods are considered for patients with significant comorbidities.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.title: Epidemiology and Pathophysiology of Fecal Incontinence
items.content: Fecal incontinence affects approximately 15% of adults in the U.S., with higher rates among older parous women. It often results from multifactorial causes, including obstetric injury to the pelvic floor.
keyPoints: Prevalence of fecal incontinence is ~15% in U.S. adults.
Up to 50% of patients also experience urinary incontinence.
Obstetric injury can lead to anatomic sphincter defects in up to 32% of women post-childbirth.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.title: Evaluation and Management of Fecal Incontinence
items.content: Fecal incontinence is a complex condition often associated with neurogenic dysfunction, surgical injury, or pelvic floor disorders. A thorough history and physical examination including digital rectal examination (DRE) are essential for initial evaluation. Weak sphincter tone on DRE and loss of the anal wink reflex may indicate neurogenic issues. Additional diagnostic studies such as anal manometry, pudendal nerve terminal motor latency (PNTML), and endoanal ultrasound can provide further insights into the underlying causes.
keyPoints: Weak sphincter tone on DRE and loss of the 'anal wink' reflex may indicate neurogenic dysfunction.
Perianal scars may represent surgical injury.
Anal manometry, PNTML, and endoanal ultrasound are useful diagnostic tools.
Pudendal nerve studies assess nerve function using a finger electrode in the anal canal.
Endoanal ultrasound evaluates sphincter muscle injury before surgical repair.
MRI's role in evaluating the sphincter muscle complex is not well-established.
Pelvic floor disorders often coexist with urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.title: Comprehensive Approach to Pelvic Floor Disorders
items.content: Patients presenting with fecal incontinence often have concurrent pelvic floor disorders, including urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse. A comprehensive evaluation should include dynamic MRI or cinedefecography to identify associated defects. Addressing all related issues is crucial for the success of surgical interventions aimed at treating fecal incontinence.
keyPoints: Fecal incontinence often coexists with urinary incontinence and pelvic organ prolapse.
Dynamic MRI or cinedefecography should be used to identify associated defects.
Surgical repair of incontinence without addressing other defects may reduce success.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.title: Management Strategies for Fecal Incontinence
items.content: Medical management of fecal incontinence involves strategies to bulk up the stool, which helps increase fecal sensation and complete evacuation. Stool bulking agents such as fiber supplementation, loperamide, diphenoxylate, and bile acid binders are used to bind the stool, resulting in more complete evacuation and decreased frequency of bowel movements. Biofeedback is recommended for all patients with fecal incontinence due to its risk-free nature and potential for improvement.
keyPoints: Stool bulking agents include fiber supplementation, loperamide, diphenoxylate, and bile acid binders.
Biofeedback helps strengthen the external sphincter muscle and should be recommended initially to all patients with fecal incontinence.
No medical or surgical therapy reduces flatus incontinence; dietary alterations and probiotics are suggested.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.title: Advanced Therapies for Fecal Incontinence
items.content: For patients with fecal incontinence, sacral nerve stimulation (SNS) is FDA-approved and indicated for those with two or more episodes of frank fecal incontinence per week. SNS has shown promising long-term results, with nearly 80% of patients experiencing a reduction in incontinence episodes by at least 50%. Alternative therapies include collagen-enhancing injectables and anal implantable self-expanding prostheses, which are options for those not suitable for sacral nerve modulation.
keyPoints: Sacral nerve stimulation (SNS) is FDA-approved for patients with two or more episodes of fecal incontinence per week.
Long-term results show nearly 80% reduction in incontinence episodes by at least 50% with SNS.
Alternative therapies include collagen-enhancing injectables and anal self-expanding prostheses.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.title: Management of Mixed Constipation and Fecal Incontinence with Transanal Irrigation
items.content: Up to 20% of patients evaluated for fecal incontinence have mixed constipation and fecal incontinence, often requiring significant laxative use. A transanal irrigation system (e.g., Peristeen by Coloplast) can assist in evacuations, reduce oral laxative dependence, and decrease incontinence episodes.
keyPoints: Up to 20% of patients with fecal incontinence have mixed constipation and fecal incontinence.
Transanal irrigation systems help reduce the need for oral laxatives and incontinence episodes.
Long-term adherence can be challenging.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.title: Diagnosis and Evaluation of Hemorrhoidal Disease
items.content: Hemorrhoidal disease is common, affecting over 1 million individuals annually in the Western world. Diagnosis relies on physical examination, including inspection for thrombosis or excoriation, digital examination, and anoscopy to assess hemorrhoid stage and location.
keyPoints: Symptomatic hemorrhoids affect >1 million individuals per year in the Western world.
Diagnosis is made through physical examination: inspection, digital exam, and anoscopy.
Differentiate between rectal prolapse and prolapsing hemorrhoids.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.title: Management Strategies for Bleeding Hemorrhoids Based on Stage and Complications
items.content: Treatment of bleeding hemorrhoids varies depending on the stage, with nonoperative therapies like rubber band ligation, infrared coagulation, and sclerotherapy being initial options. Surgical methods include excisional hemorrhoidectomy, bipolar electrocautery ligation (LigaSure), transhemorrhoidal dearterialization (THD), and stapled hemorrhoidectomy (PPH). Each method has specific indications and potential complications.
keyPoints: Nonoperative therapies: rubber band ligation, infrared coagulation, sclerotherapy with 1–2 mL of sodium tetra-decyl sulfate
Surgical options: excisional hemorrhoidectomy (Milligan-Morgan), LigaSure, THD, PPH
Complications to avoid in immunocompromised patients or those with active proctitis
Acute complications include pain, infection, recurrent bleeding, urinary retention
Late complications can involve fecal incontinence and anal stenosis due to overzealous excision
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Hemorrhoidal Disease

items.title: Considerations for Acutely Thrombosed Hemorrhoids and Postoperative Outcomes
items.content: Acute thrombosed hemorrhoids can be managed with elliptical excision within 48 hours, often at the bedside. Surgical treatments aim to improve bleeding, pain, and recurrence rates, while avoiding complications like fecal incontinence or anal stenosis.
keyPoints: Acutely thrombosed hemorrhoids: excised within first 48 hours
Postoperative outcomes focus on reducing bleeding, pain, and recurrence
Sutured hemorrhoidectomy may cause more postoperative pain and bleeding
Stapled hemorrhoidectomy associated with less discomfort but potential complications from the stapling device
THD uses ultrasound guidance to reduce hemorrhoidal engorgement
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Hemorrhoidal Disease

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Anorectal Abscesses
items.content: Anorectal abscesses are more common in men than women, with a 3:1 ratio. They predominantly occur between the third to fifth decades of life. Immunocompromised individuals, such as those with diabetes, hematologic disorders, IBD, or HIV, have an increased prevalence and should be considered for recurrent infections.
keyPoints: Incidence is higher in men (3:1 ratio).
Peak incidence occurs in the third to fifth decade of life.
More prevalent in immunocompromised patients (e.g., diabetes, hematologic disorders, IBD, HIV).
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Anorectal Abscesses
items.content: Anorectal abscesses present with perianal pain and fever. Physical examination typically reveals a fluctuant area, and elevated WBC count is common. Drainage is the gold standard treatment; uncomplicated cases can be managed in-office, while complicated or immunocompromised patients require operating room drainage under anesthesia to prevent necrotizing fasciitis. Antibiotics are limited to immunocompromised individuals or those with cellulitis.
keyPoints: Presentation includes perianal pain and fever.
Physical exam shows a fluctuant area; elevated WBC count is common.
Drainage is the gold standard treatment.
Office drainage for uncomplicated cases; operating room required for complicated or immunocompromised patients.
Antibiotics are limited to specific cases (immunocompromised, cellulitis).
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.title: Incidence and Origin of Anal Fistulas
items.content: Anal fistulas are a complication of perianal abscesses, with an incidence paralleling that of the abscess. Most fistulas arise cryptoglandularly, but a significant minority is associated with systemic conditions.
keyPoints: Incidence of fistulizing perianal disease: 1 in 10,000 individuals
30–40% of anorectal abscesses develop into anal fistulas
Cryptoglandular origin accounts for the majority; 10% associated with IBD, tuberculosis, malignancy, or radiation
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.title: Classification and Evaluation of Anal Fistulas
items.content: Anal fistulas are classified based on their relationship to the anal sphincter muscles. MRI is a valuable tool for preoperative evaluation, with Goodsall's rule aiding in predicting fistula tract paths.
keyPoints: 70% intersphincteric, 23% transsphincteric, 5% suprasphincteric, and 2% extrasphincteric classification of anal fistulas
Preoperative MRI identifies tracts with 80% accuracy in complex cases
Goodsall's rule: posterior external fistula enters at the posterior midline; anterior fistula enters nearest crypt. Exceptions for fistulas >3 cm from the anal verge
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of individuals with diverticulosis will develop acute diverticular disease?
items.explanation: Studies have shown that approximately 5% of individuals with diverticulosis will develop acute diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Where are diverticula most commonly encountered in the colon?
items.explanation: Diverticula are most commonly encountered in the sigmoid colon.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which type of diverticulum is most common in the colon?
options: Neither, as they do not occur in the colon
items.explanation: The pseudodiverticulum is the type most commonly seen in the colon.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which genes have been associated with early-onset, severe diverticular disease according to genome-wide associations?
items.explanation: GWAs have identified LAMB4 and TNFSF15 variants to be associated with early-onset, severe diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is best for diagnosing acute uncomplicated diverticulitis?
options: Contrast-enhanced abdominal and pelvic CT scan
Nuclear medicine tagged red cell scan
items.explanation: A contrast-enhanced abdominal and pelvic CT scan is best for diagnosing acute uncomplicated diverticulitis.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is the rate of colonic ischemia with highly selective coil embolization?
items.explanation: The rate of colonic ischemia with highly selective coil embolization is <10%.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which treatment approach can be used to manage diverticular bleeding if the site can be localized?
options: Nuclear medicine tagged red cell scan
items.explanation: Colonoscopic ligation with banding can be used to manage diverticular bleeding if the site can be localized.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is indicated for patients who have received >10 units of blood in the context of surgical resection?
items.explanation: A higher anastomotic leak rate has been reported in patients who received >10 units of blood.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended for routine use in uncomplicated diverticular disease?
items.explanation: Routine use of antibiotics does not reduce time to symptom resolution or risk of complications in uncomplicated diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.question: In which patients is antibiotic treatment recommended for diverticular disease?
options: Patients with extensive inflammation on radiographic studies
Patients without any risk factors for progression
Patients who have been symptom-free for over 5 days
items.explanation: Antibiotics are recommended for patients with extensive inflammation, at risk of disease progression, or complicated diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.question: What is the usual course duration for antibiotics in uncomplicated acute diverticulitis?
items.explanation: The typical antibiotic treatment duration for uncomplicated acute diverticulitis is 5 days.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.question: Which medication has been shown to be beneficial in reducing symptoms and disease recurrence in patients with SUDD, according to randomized clinical trials?
items.explanation: Randomized clinical trials have shown mesalazine to be beneficial at reducing symptoms and disease recurrence in patients with SUDD.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.question: What is the recommended timing for a colonoscopy after an episode of uncomplicated diverticular disease?
items.explanation: Colonoscopy should be considered ~6 weeks after the first episode of uncomplicated diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.question: What is the prevalence of colon cancer in patients with uncomplicated diverticular disease?
items.explanation: The overall prevalence of colon cancer in patients with uncomplicated diverticular disease is low (<2%).
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.question: Which trial demonstrated that elective surgical resection for recurrent SUDD was more cost-effective at 5 years compared to conservative management?
items.explanation: The DIRECT trial showed that elective surgical resection was more cost-effective at 5 years compared to conservative management for recurrent SUDD.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is the increased risk associated with laparoscopic washout and drainage without diversion in Hinchey III patients?
options: Threefold increased risk of recurrent peritonitis requiring reoperation
Higher mortality rate compared to open procedures
items.explanation: Laparoscopic washout and drainage without diversion in Hinchey III patients is associated with a threefold increased risk of recurrent peritonitis requiring reoperation.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What are the benefits of minimally invasive resection over open surgical techniques for diverticular disease?
options: Increased narcotic use and longer hospital stay
Early discharge, less narcotic use, fewer postoperative complications, earlier return to work
Higher rate of conversion to an open procedure
items.explanation: Minimally invasive resection offers benefits such as early discharge, less narcotic use, fewer postoperative complications, and earlier return to work compared to open surgical techniques.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended management for Hinchey stage Ia disease?
options: Antibiotic therapy only or followed by resection with anastomosis after further workup and symptom resolution
Percutaneous drainage followed by resection with anastomosis
items.explanation: Management of Hinchey stage Ia disease may include antibiotic therapy only or resection with anastomosis after further workup and symptom resolution.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM
items.question: What is associated with twice the incidence of recurrence in patients following surgical resection for diverticular disease?
options: Retained segment of diseased rectosigmoid colon
items.explanation: A retained segment of diseased rectosigmoid colon is associated with twice the incidence of recurrence.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.subtopic: SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM
items.question: Which group is more commonly affected by rectal prolapse?
items.explanation: Rectal prolapse is six times more common in women than in men, peaking in those over 60.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.subtopic: SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM
items.question: What is a common association of rectal prolapse in children?
items.explanation: About 20% of children with rectal prolapse will have cystic fibrosis.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.question: Which surgical approach for rectal prolapse has been associated with lower recurrence rates?
items.explanation: Transabdominal approaches have been associated with lower recurrence rates.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.question: What is the mainstay of therapy for rectal prolapse?
items.explanation: Surgical correction of rectal prolapse is the mainstay of therapy.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.question: What percentage of patients experience successful resolution of symptoms from biofeedback for internal rectal prolapse?
items.explanation: Only one-third (approximately 33%) of patients will have successful resolution of symptoms from biofeedback.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.question: Which medical condition is known to contribute to the development of fecal incontinence?
items.explanation: Medical conditions such as obstetric injury are known to contribute to the development of fecal incontinence.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.question: Which condition listed under 'Neurologic Disorders' is characterized by a loss of proprioception and deep tendon reflexes, often leading to fecal incontinence?
items.explanation: Tabes dorsalis is characterized by loss of proprioception and deep tendon reflexes, contributing to fecal incontinence.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.question: Which test evaluates the function of nerves innervating the anal canal using a finger electrode placed in the anal canal?
items.explanation: Pudendal nerve terminal motor latency (PNTML) uses a finger electrode to evaluate the function of nerves innervating the anal canal.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.question: Which imaging modality is mentioned as having an unestablished role in evaluating the sphincter muscle complex for fecal incontinence treatment planning?
items.explanation: MRI's role in evaluating the sphincter muscle complex for fecal incontinence treatment planning is not well-established.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a strategy used in the medical management of fecal incontinence?
options: Stool bulking agents such as fiber supplementation and loperamide.
Biofeedback therapy to strengthen external sphincter muscles.
Overlapping sphincteroplasty for isolated sphincter defects.
Use of stem cells to increase the bulk of the sphincter muscles.
items.explanation: Stem cell use is currently being tested and not a standard medical management strategy.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.question: Which therapy for fecal incontinence involves stimulating the tibial nerve to feedback to the third sacral nerve root?
options: Percutaneous or transcutaneous tibial nerve stimulation (PTNS or TTNS).
items.explanation: PTNS and TTNS involve stimulating the tibial nerve to feedback to the third sacral nerve root.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.question: What is a limitation of overlapping sphincteroplasty in treating fecal incontinence?
options: It has no risk and always improves symptoms.
Long-term results have been poor with a 50% failure rate over 5 years.
It is the only FDA-approved treatment for fecal incontinence.
items.explanation: Overlapping sphincteroplasty has long-term results that are poor, with a high failure rate over time.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.question: Which intervention has shown promising results in managing mixed constipation and fecal incontinence by reducing the need for oral laxatives?
options: Use of a transanal irrigation system (Peristeen by Coloplast)
items.explanation: The passage mentions that the use of a transanal irrigation system has shown promising results.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.question: What is one drawback mentioned for using the transanal irrigation system in managing mixed constipation and fecal incontinence?
items.explanation: The passage states that a drawback is long-term adherence to the irrigations.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.question: What dietary factor is associated with the development of symptomatic hemorrhoids in developed countries?
items.explanation: The passage associates a typical low-fiber, high-fat diet with the development of symptomatic hemorrhoids.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.question: Which nonoperative therapy for bleeding hemorrhoids involves placing bands around engorged tissue to cause ischemia and fibrosis?
items.explanation: Rubber band ligation involves placing bands around the engorged tissue to cause ischemia and fibrosis.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Hemorrhoidal Disease

items.question: What is a potential complication of sclerotherapy for hemorrhoids if the anal canal is injected circumferentially?
items.explanation: Circumferential injection during sclerotherapy can lead to anal stenosis.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Hemorrhoidal Disease

items.question: Which surgical procedure for hemorrhoids is associated with less postoperative discomfort but does not remove anal skin tags?
items.explanation: Stapled hemorrhoidectomy is associated with less discomfort but does not remove anal skin tags.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Hemorrhoidal Disease

items.question: What is the most common location for an anorectal abscess?
items.explanation: Anorectal abscesses are perianal in 40–50% of patients, making it the most common location.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.question: Which patient population is more likely to develop an anorectal abscess?
items.explanation: The development of a perianal abscess is more common in men than women by a ratio of 3:1.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.question: What diagnostic procedure has an accuracy of 80% for determining incomplete drainage of an anorectal abscess?
items.explanation: A CT scan or MRI has an accuracy of 80% in determining incomplete drainage.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.question: What is the estimated incidence of fistulizing perianal disease?
items.explanation: The incidence of fistulizing perianal disease is estimated to be 1 in 10,000 individuals.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.question: What percentage of anal fistulas are classified as transsphincteric?
items.explanation: Transsphincteric fistulas account for 23% of all anal fistulas.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.question: Which imaging modality is recommended preoperatively to evaluate complex fistulas?
items.explanation: A preoperative MRI is used to identify tracts with an accuracy of 80% in cases of complex fistulas.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: The rectum is commonly affected by diverticula.
items.explanation: The rectum is always spared from diverticula.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: Obesity is a risk factor for diverticular bleeding.
items.explanation: Additional risk factors for diverticular bleeding include obesity.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Symptomatic colitis-associated diverticulosis (SCAD) occurs in more than 1% of patients with diverticulosis.
items.explanation: SCAD is uncommon and occurs in less than 1% of patients with diverticulosis.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: In complicated diverticular disease, symptoms may include signs of peritonitis indicating a perforation.
items.explanation: Symptoms of complicated diverticular disease can include signs of peritonitis due to a perforation.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.statement: Antibiotics are recommended for all patients with symptomatic diverticular disease.
items.explanation: Antibiotics are not routinely used in uncomplicated cases but are reserved for specific high-risk or complicated cases.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS
items.statement: A fiber-enriched diet is beneficial in managing symptomatic diverticular disease.
items.explanation: Increasing dietary fiber can help decrease colonic transit time and prevent increased intraluminal pressure.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Medical Management of Diverticular Disease | ANTIBIOTICS

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.statement: Probiotics alone have been shown to be beneficial for preventing recurrence of diverticulitis.
items.explanation: A systematic review was unable to show any benefit to the use of probiotics alone.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.subtopic: DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY
items.statement: Rifaximin is associated with a higher frequency of recurrent symptoms from uncomplicated diverticular disease compared to fiber alone.
items.explanation: Rifaximin, when compared to fiber alone, is associated with 30% less frequent recurrent symptoms.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIET AND OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES | COLONOSCOPY

items.statement: Patients on immunosuppressive therapy have a fivefold greater risk of perforation during recurrent attacks.
items.explanation: Patients on immunosuppressive therapy, in chronic renal failure, or with collagen-vascular disease do indeed have a fivefold greater risk of perforation.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: No anastomosis should be attempted in Hinchey stage IV disease or in the presence of fecal peritonitis.
items.explanation: In Hinchey stage IV disease or with fecal peritonitis, no anastomosis is recommended due to high risk.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM
items.statement: Rectal prolapse is more common in men than women.
items.explanation: Rectal prolapse is six times more common in women than in men.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.statement: Full-thickness rectal prolapse is often associated with bilateral pudendal nerve injury.
items.explanation: Bilateral pudendal nerve injury is significantly more identified with full-thickness prolapse and fecal incontinence.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.statement: Fecal incontinence is more prevalent among older parous women.
items.explanation: A higher incidence of fecal incontinence is seen among older parous women.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.statement: The internal anal sphincter is under voluntary control.
items.explanation: The internal anal sphincter is not under voluntary control; it is innervated by the intestinal myenteric plexus.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.statement: Fecal incontinence often occurs without any associated urinary symptoms.
items.explanation: The majority of patients with fecal incontinence also have some degree of urinary incontinence.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.subtopic: Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous
items.statement: Endoanal ultrasound is used to evaluate the extent of injury to sphincter muscles before surgical repair.
items.explanation: Endoanal ultrasound evaluates the extent of injury to the sphincter muscles prior to surgery.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Neurologic Disorders | Skeletal Muscle Disorders | Miscellaneous

items.statement: There is no medical or surgical therapy to reduce incontinence of flatus.
items.explanation: Dietary alterations and probiotics are the only remedies for reducing flatus incontinence.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.statement: Sacral nerve stimulation (SNS) is indicated for patients with less than two episodes of fecal incontinence per week.
items.explanation: SNS is indicated for patients with two or more episodes of fecal incontinence per week.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Fecal Incontinence

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.statement: Hemorrhoidal disease is more prevalent in developing countries.
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence of hemorrhoidal disease is less in developing countries.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE
items.statement: Pain from hemorrhoids is more commonly described as a dull ache due to engorgement.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that pain, if present, is described as a dull ache from engorgement of the hemorrhoidal tissue.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · MIXED CONSTIPATION AND FECAL INCONTINENCE | HEMORRHOIDAL DISEASE

items.statement: Sensation begins at the dentate line, making all procedures performed without discomfort.
items.explanation: Procedures can be performed without discomfort because sensation starts at the dentate line.

items.statement: Emergent hemorrhoidectomy for bleeding hemorrhoids is associated with a lower complication rate.
items.explanation: Emergent hemorrhoidectomy is actually associated with a higher complication rate.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Hemorrhoidal Disease

items.statement: Antibiotics are routinely used for all patients with anorectal abscesses.
items.explanation: The role of antibiotics is limited and only warranted in immunocompromised patients or those with obvious cellulitis.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.statement: A perianal abscess can be drained in the office if uncomplicated.
items.explanation: Office drainage of an uncomplicated anorectal abscess may suffice.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · ANORECTAL ABSCESS | Anorectal Abscess

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.statement: An anal fistula is a communication between an abscess cavity and the skin surface.
items.explanation: An anal fistula is defined as a communication of an abscess cavity with an identifiable internal opening within the anal canal.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.statement: Goodsall's rule applies to all external fistulas regardless of their distance from the anal verge.
items.explanation: A fistula exiting >3 cm from the anal verge may not obey Goodsall's rule due to a possible complicated upward extension.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Chronic low-grade inflammation leads to dysmotility in diverticulosis.
items.reason: Chronic low-grade inflammation is thought to play a key role in neuronal degeneration, leading to dysmotility and high intraluminal pressure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: The majority of patients with diverticulosis experience symptoms.
items.reason: Studies have shown that ~5% of individuals with diverticulosis will develop acute diverticular disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the majority of affected individuals do not experience symptoms; however, the reason is true regarding the development of acute diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders | DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: A segmental resection of the colon may be undertaken if hemostasis is not achieved.
items.reason: This approach can prevent delayed bleeding and perforation in patients on chronic anticoagulation and immunosuppression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.assertion: The Hinchey classification system is used to predict outcomes following surgical management of uncomplicated diverticular disease.
items.reason: It includes stages for phlegmon or early abscess development in complicated diverticular disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the Hinchey classification is used for complicated, not uncomplicated, diverticular disease.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2)

items.assertion: Laparoscopic surgery for refractory or complicated diverticular disease improves quality of life.
items.reason: The LASER trial demonstrated improved quality of life and a reduction in recurrent symptoms by 50% compared to conservative treatment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Robotic surgery resection for complicated diverticular disease has a higher rate of conversion to open procedures.
items.reason: Robotic surgery is associated with a lower rate of conversion to an open procedure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; robotic surgery actually has a lower rate of conversion to open procedures.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM
items.assertion: Rectal prolapse is due to early internal rectal intussusception.
items.reason: Only 1 of 38 patients with internal prolapse followed for >5 years developed full-thickness prolapse.
items.explanation: The assertion that rectal prolapse results from early internal rectal intussusception is false, as the reason shows a low progression rate to full-thickness prolapse.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · SURGICAL MANAGEMENT OF DIVERTICULAR DISEASE (part 2) | RECTAL PROLAPSE (PROCIDENTIA) | COMMON DISEASES OF THE ANORECTUM

items.assertion: Ventral rectopexy does not require sigmoid resection.
items.reason: This repair has been shown to have improved pelvic floor functional results over other abdominal repairs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of why ventral rectopexy does not require sigmoid resection.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.assertion: Obstetric injury can result in tearing of muscle fibers anteriorly.
items.reason: This results in an obvious anterior defect on endoanal ultrasound.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the anterior defect observed.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · Rectal Prolapse | FECAL INCONTINENCE

items.subtopic: FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
items.assertion: Continuous drainage at 1 month following treatment of a perianal abscess suggests an anal fistula.
items.reason: This indicates the presence of a persistent communication between the abscess cavity and the anal canal.
items.explanation: The persistence of drainage is indicative of a fistula, which is a communication with an identifiable internal opening within the anal canal.
items.reference: Diverticular Disease and Common Anorectal Disorders · FISTULA IN ANO | Fistula in Ano
